subtopic_detail: Hand foot and mouth disease counselling
stem: You are the GP seeing a 3-year-old girl whose mother is worried she has chickenpox. Two days ago the child developed a low-grade fever and refused breakfast. This morning small grey-white blisters appeared on her palms, soles and the inside of her cheeks. Her older brother attends the same childcare centre. The family is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander and the girl is fully immunised including two doses of varicella vaccine. Mum asks when she can go back to childcare.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6°C, HR 110, RR 24, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Pinpoint vesicles on a non-erythematous base on hands, feet and buccal mucosa; trunk and face spared; alert and drinking small volumes

lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice regarding return to childcare?

options.text: Intramuscular adrenaline 10 micrograms/kg into the lateral thigh and observe response over 5 minutes
options.rationale: The Staying Healthy 5th edition advises exclusion of children with HFMD until the blisters have dried, with the child also clinically well; this aligns the duration to actual infectivity and clinical state rather than a fixed day count. (Source: Staying Healthy 5th edition NHMRC)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg infused over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring in HDU
options.rationale: HFMD is not on the standard national notifiable list and is managed by exclusion plus hygiene; PHU notification is only triggered in clusters of severe disease such as suspected EV71-associated brainstem encephalitis. (Source: CDNA Series of National Guidelines)

options.text: IV aminophylline 10 mg/kg load over 60 minutes on the general paediatric ward with telemetry only
options.rationale: HFMD does carry an exclusion period in Australian early childhood settings; absence of fever does not change the recommendation while the vesicles are still moist. (Source: Staying Healthy 5th edition NHMRC)

options.text: Switch to high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min FiO2 0.5 and reassess at 30 minutes off bronchodilator
options.rationale: There is no mandated 21 day exclusion for HFMD; this length applies to no notifiable childhood infection currently listed in the NHMRC schedule. (Source: Staying Healthy 5th edition NHMRC)

options.text: Switch corticosteroid route to IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg slow bolus and continue current nebulisers
options.rationale: This is the rule for varicella, not for hand foot and mouth disease; the vesicles in HFMD do not crust like chickenpox and prolonged exclusion is not required. (Source: Staying Healthy: Preventing infectious diseases in early childhood education and care services)

explanation.summary: Hand foot and mouth disease is a common coxsackievirus or enterovirus rash with grey-white vesicles on the palms, soles and oral mucosa. The Staying Healthy 5th edition recommends exclusion until the blisters have dried; there is no fixed day count and crusting is not part of the natural history. Hygiene measures and surface cleaning at the childcare centre are the main control strategies, with EV71 surveillance flagged separately for severe disease.
key_points: Exclude from childcare until blisters have dried, not until they have crusted.
There is no fixed day count for return; clinical wellness is required in addition.
Notification is only triggered in clusters with severe neurological involvement suggesting EV71.
explanation.pearls: If a child with HFMD develops ataxia, drowsiness or myoclonic jerks within the first 5 days, escalate urgently because enterovirus 71 brainstem encephalitis is a public health priority.

subtopic_detail: Scarlet fever and rheumatic fever prevention
stem: You are the rural GP seeing a 7-year-old Aboriginal boy from a remote community presenting with two days of sore throat, fever and a rough sandpaper-like rash that started on his neck and spread to his trunk. He has a strawberry tongue and tender anterior cervical nodes. He has no penicillin allergy and is well enough to be managed as an outpatient with reliable follow up through the community clinic. His mother is happy to give oral medication.

data_table.Examination: Erythematous oropharynx with tonsillar exudate, palpable petechial rash with sandpaper texture sparing the face, strawberry tongue, no joint swelling, no carditis
data_table.Investigations: Rapid throat swab unavailable; clinical diagnosis

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 15 mg/kg twice daily for an abbreviated five-day course
options.rationale: Five days is enough to settle symptoms but is insufficient to eradicate Group A Streptococcus from the pharynx and so does not reliably prevent acute rheumatic fever. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg twice daily for a seven-day course with supportive care
options.rationale: Amoxicillin is an acceptable alternative when adherence to penicillin V dosing is a concern, but the AU rheumatic-fever prevention regimens specify a full ten-day duration, not seven. (Source: RHDAustralia guideline)
source_refs: RHDAustralia: Australian guideline for prevention, diagnosis and management of ARF and RHD

options.text: Symptomatic care only with paracetamol and clinic review in 48 hours if not better
options.rationale: In a child from a high acute rheumatic fever risk group, withholding antibiotics is unsafe because rheumatic fever can follow even mild pharyngitis. (Source: RHDAustralia guideline)
source_refs: RHDAustralia: Australian guideline for prevention, diagnosis and management of ARF and RHD

options.text: Oral roxithromycin 4 mg/kg twice daily for ten days with topical throat lozenges
options.rationale: Macrolides are reserved for documented penicillin hypersensitivity; in penicillin-tolerant patients they are not first-line because of increasing macrolide-resistant Group A Streptococcus. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 15 mg/kg twice daily for a full ten-day course
options.rationale: In Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children living in remote communities a full ten-day course of phenoxymethylpenicillin (or single dose IM benzathine benzylpenicillin) is required to prevent acute rheumatic fever, even when symptoms resolve early. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Scarlet fever in an Aboriginal child from a remote community sits in the highest-risk group for acute rheumatic fever. The cornerstone of prevention is eradication of Group A Streptococcus from the pharynx with a full ten-day course of oral phenoxymethylpenicillin or a single dose of intramuscular benzathine benzylpenicillin. Shorter courses settle symptoms but leave organisms behind and do not prevent ARF. Macrolides are reserved for true penicillin allergy.
key_points: Phenoxymethylpenicillin 15 mg/kg twice daily for ten days is first-line.
Single dose IM benzathine benzylpenicillin is an alternative when adherence is uncertain.
Shorter courses settle symptoms but do not prevent rheumatic fever.
explanation.pearls: In any Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander child with pharyngitis, treat group A streptococcal infection as a 10-day course or single benzathine dose; do not stop at 5 days even if the child is asymptomatic by day 3.

sources.label: RHDAustralia: Australian guideline for prevention, diagnosis and management of ARF and RHD

stem: You are the paediatric outpatient registrar reviewing a 14-month-old boy referred by his GP for bow legs and delayed walking. He was exclusively breastfed until 12 months and was not given vitamin D supplements. He spends most of his day indoors and his mother veils for cultural reasons. The family lives in Melbourne and the child has darkly pigmented skin. He has no chronic illnesses, no diarrhoea, and his diet is now mostly cow's milk with very little meat or fortified cereal.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7°C, HR 112, RR 24, weight on 25th centile, length on 10th centile
data_table.Examination: Frontal bossing, wrist widening, costochondral beading, valgus deformity of the lower limbs, hypotonia, no fractures
data_table.Investigations: Calcium 1.95 mmol/L, phosphate 0.7 mmol/L, ALP 1200 U/L, 25-OH vitamin D 18 nmol/L, PTH elevated, wrist X-ray: cupping and fraying of distal radius

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Oral cholecalciferol 1000 IU daily for three months without calcium supplementation
options.rationale: 1000 units daily is a maintenance dose; it is too low for established rickets, which requires a treatment dose for several weeks. (Source: APEG vitamin D position statement)
source_refs: APEG: Vitamin D and health in children

options.text: Oral cholecalciferol 3000 IU daily for 12 weeks plus elemental calcium 500 mg daily
options.rationale: Treatment-dose vitamin D (1000 to 2000 units daily under 1 month, 3000 units daily 1-12 months, or 5000 units daily over 12 months) for 12 weeks, combined with adequate calcium intake, is the Australian guideline approach to symptomatic rickets. (Source: APEG vitamin D position statement)
source_refs: APEG: Vitamin D and health in children

options.text: Refer to orthopaedics for surgical correction of genu varum before further medical workup
options.rationale: Bow legs in nutritional rickets correct over months once vitamin D and calcium are repleted; surgery is not the first step. (Source: APEG vitamin D position statement)
source_refs: APEG: Vitamin D and health in children

options.text: Single oral stoss dose of 300000 IU cholecalciferol with bloods rechecked at two weeks
options.rationale: High-dose stoss therapy has been associated with hypercalcaemia and is not the AU first-line approach; daily dosing is preferred where adherence allows. (Source: APEG vitamin D position statement)
source_refs: APEG: Vitamin D and health in children

options.text: Intravenous calcium gluconate 0.5 mL/kg over 10 minutes and admit for cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: IV calcium is reserved for symptomatic hypocalcaemia (seizures, tetany, prolonged QT); this child has mild biochemical hypocalcaemia and no acute cardiac or neuromuscular features. (Source: RCH CPG: Hypocalcaemia)

explanation.summary: Nutritional rickets in Australia is most commonly seen in dark-skinned children with limited sunlight exposure, prolonged exclusive breastfeeding without supplementation, and low dietary calcium. Diagnosis combines hypocalcaemia, hypophosphataemia, elevated ALP and PTH, low 25-OH vitamin D, plus radiographic metaphyseal cupping and fraying. Treatment is several weeks of cholecalciferol with adequate calcium intake; bony deformities correct over months without orthopaedic intervention.
key_points: Treatment dose cholecalciferol 1000 to 5000 units daily for 12 weeks depending on age.
Calcium 500 mg elemental daily is needed alongside vitamin D in established rickets.
ALP and 25-OH D are followed at 3 months to confirm response.
explanation.pearls: In suspected rickets, an ALP above 1000 U/L with a 25-OH vitamin D below 25 nmol/L is highly suggestive; check PTH to exclude renal forms.

sources.label: APEG: Vitamin D and health in children

stem: You are the GP seeing a 4-month-old formula-fed boy whose parents are worried about ongoing crying, regurgitation and intermittent loose mucousy stools, occasionally with streaks of fresh blood. Symptoms began at 6 weeks, around the time he switched from breast to standard cow milk formula. He has mild eczema on the cheeks. He is alert, playful and feeding well, but weight gain has slowed from the 50th centile to the 15th centile over six weeks.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9°C, HR 130, RR 32, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Alert, well-hydrated, mild facial eczema, soft non-tender abdomen, normal anal tone, no anal fissure
data_table.Investigations: Faecal occult blood positive, FBC normal, no eosinophilia, no skin prick or specific IgE testing performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Switch to a lactose-free cow milk based formula and review symptoms in four weeks
options.rationale: Lactose-free formula still contains intact cow milk protein and does not treat protein-induced colitis. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines: Infant feeding and allergy prevention

options.text: Refer for specific IgE testing to cow milk protein before any change of formula trial
options.rationale: Non-IgE mediated cow milk protein allergy is a clinical diagnosis with normal specific IgE; testing before a dietary trial delays treatment and is rarely diagnostic in this phenotype. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines: Infant feeding and allergy prevention

options.text: Switch to a soy-based infant formula and review symptoms in two weeks at the clinic
options.rationale: Up to one in three infants with cow milk protein allergy are also intolerant of soy protein; ASCIA does not recommend soy formula as a first-line substitute under 6 months. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines: Infant feeding and allergy prevention

options.text: Thicken current formula and start omeprazole 1 mg/kg once daily before any formula change
options.rationale: Acid suppression treats reflux oesophagitis but does not address the immune-mediated colitis suggested by streaks of blood and slowing weight gain. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastro-oesophageal reflux)

options.text: Trial extensively hydrolysed casein formula for two to four weeks with clinical reassessment
options.rationale: Non-IgE mediated cow's milk protein allergy in a young infant with proctocolitis features is managed by a trial of an extensively hydrolysed formula; symptomatic improvement within two to four weeks supports the diagnosis. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines: Infant feeding and allergy prevention)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines: Infant feeding and allergy prevention

explanation.summary: Non-IgE mediated cow milk protein allergy commonly presents in young formula-fed infants with crying, vomiting, mucousy bloody stools and faltering growth. Diagnosis is clinical: a 2 to 4 week trial of an extensively hydrolysed formula should resolve symptoms, with a planned milk challenge between 9 and 12 months. Soy formula is not first-line under 6 months because of cross-reactivity, and lactose-free formula does not remove the offending protein.
key_points: Extensively hydrolysed casein formula is first-line for proven or suspected non-IgE CMPA under 6 months.
Soy formula is avoided as first-line under 6 months due to cross-reactivity.
Specific IgE testing is normal in non-IgE phenotypes and should not gate the trial.
explanation.pearls: Blood-streaked mucous stools plus eczema and slowing weight gain in an infant just after the breast-to-formula switch is classic non-IgE cow milk protein allergy; expect symptoms to resolve within 2 to 4 weeks of extensively hydrolysed formula.

sources.label: ASCIA Guidelines: Infant feeding and allergy prevention

stem: You are the GP reviewing a 7-day-old term baby boy with three days of bilateral sticky eyes and increasing eyelid swelling. He was born by spontaneous vaginal delivery to a 19-year-old mother whose antenatal STI screening was incomplete. The baby is feeding and gaining weight well, has no fever and no respiratory distress. Examination shows marked tarsal injection with mucopurulent discharge and mild eyelid oedema.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9°C, HR 140, RR 44, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Bilateral mucopurulent conjunctival discharge, no corneal involvement on fluorescein, no preauricular nodes, clear chest
data_table.Investigations: Eye swab for chlamydia PCR and bacterial culture obtained

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial therapy while results are pending?

options.text: Saline eye toilet four times daily and clinical review in 48 hours if not improving
options.rationale: Watchful management without antimicrobials is appropriate for chemical conjunctivitis in the first 24 hours of life, not for a day-7 mucopurulent picture with high suspicion of chlamydia. (Source: STIs in gay men action group AU and Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Single intramuscular ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg dose with saline eye toilet four times daily
options.rationale: Single-dose ceftriaxone is the regimen for suspected gonococcal ophthalmia neonatorum (day 1 to 5, hyperacute purulent discharge); this presentation pattern is typical of chlamydia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Topical chloramphenicol drops six times daily for seven days with saline eye toilet
options.rationale: Chloramphenicol drops cover bacterial conjunctivitis but do not eradicate chlamydia from the nasopharynx, leaving the baby at risk of pneumonitis at 4 to 12 weeks of age. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral azithromycin 20 mg/kg as a single dose with saline eye toilet four times daily
options.rationale: Azithromycin single dose is the standard for adult genital chlamydia but is not the AU regimen for neonatal chlamydial conjunctivitis; the 14-day erythromycin course also reduces pneumonitis risk and infantile pyloric stenosis risk is similar with either macrolide. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral erythromycin 50 mg/kg per day in four divided doses for a total of 14 days
options.rationale: Neonatal chlamydial conjunctivitis at day 5 to 14 of life is treated with systemic oral erythromycin for 14 days because topical therapy alone does not prevent later chlamydial pneumonitis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Neonatal chlamydial conjunctivitis typically presents from day 5 to day 14 of life with mucopurulent discharge in a well-appearing baby. Systemic erythromycin for 14 days is the standard Australian regimen because topical therapy alone does not eradicate organisms from the nasopharynx and predisposes to chlamydial pneumonitis at 4 to 12 weeks. Maternal partner notification, infant pyloric stenosis counselling and contact tracing are integral parts of management.
key_points: Oral erythromycin 50 mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses for 14 days is first-line.
Topical therapy alone is inadequate because of nasopharyngeal colonisation.
Counsel parents about the small risk of infantile pyloric stenosis with macrolides under 6 weeks.
explanation.pearls: Sticky eyes in a 1-2 week old baby with mucopurulent discharge and a well-appearing infant should be treated as chlamydia until PCR proves otherwise; do not stop at topical chloramphenicol.

sources.label: ASHM Sexually transmissible infections in primary care manual

stem: You are the rural GP seeing a 2-year-old girl who pulled a cup of freshly boiled tea onto her chest 30 minutes ago. The mother removed wet clothing and ran cool tap water for 20 minutes before driving in. The child is alert and crying intermittently but consolable. Examination shows a moist erythematous area with intact blisters across the anterior chest and right anterolateral abdomen. There is no facial, perineal or circumferential involvement.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8°C, HR 130, RR 26, SpO2 99% on room air, cap refill 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Estimated 6% total body surface area partial-thickness scald to anterior chest and right flank, blistered with serous fluid, very painful to touch, no charring, no inhalational injury signs

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: IV crystalloid by Parkland formula at 4 mL/kg per percent TBSA over the first 24 hours
options.rationale: Parkland-style fluid resuscitation is started for paediatric burns of at least 10% TBSA; a 6% scald is below that threshold but still warrants oral hydration and transfer. (Source: ANZBA Burn Patient Management)

options.text: Burst blisters with a sterile needle, dress with paraffin gauze, observe in primary care
options.rationale: Deroofing intact blisters in the community is not advised; it increases pain, infection risk and contamination before burns-service assessment. (Source: ANZBA Burn Patient Management)

options.text: Cling film cover, intranasal fentanyl 1.5 micrograms/kg, urgent paediatric burns transfer
options.rationale: ANZBA criteria require referral to a designated paediatric burns service for any partial-thickness burn over 5% TBSA in a child under 16; the AU pre-transfer cover is loose cling film (not circumferential), with effective analgesia. (Source: ANZBA Burn Patient Management 4th edition)

options.text: Continue cool running water for a further 30 minutes and reassess depth and area after
options.rationale: Cooling beyond 20 minutes after a scald is not beneficial and increases hypothermia risk, particularly in a young child with a 6% surface area injury. (Source: ANZBA Burn Patient Management)

options.text: Silver sulfadiazine cream, non-adherent dressing, oral paracetamol, review the next day
options.rationale: Silver sulfadiazine should not be applied before assessment by the receiving burns service because it stains the wound and complicates depth assessment; outpatient management of a 6% burn in a 2-year-old is also outside primary-care scope. (Source: ANZBA Burn Patient Management)

explanation.summary: ANZBA criteria mandate referral of any partial-thickness burn over 5% total body surface area in a child to a designated paediatric burns service, and any burn involving the face, hands, feet, perineum, joints or circumferential limb burns regardless of size. Pre-transfer wound care is cling film loosely applied, not circumferential, with strong analgesia; topical antimicrobials should be delayed until the receiving service has assessed depth. Parkland-style IV resuscitation is reserved for burns 10% or greater.
key_points: Refer any partial-thickness burn over 5% TBSA in a child.
Cling film loosely applied is the pre-transfer dressing of choice.
Parkland-style IV resuscitation begins at 10% TBSA, not at 5%.
explanation.pearls: In a child with a scald, calculate the burn area using the child's palmar surface (~1% TBSA) and use ANZBA criteria; never apply silver sulfadiazine before the burns unit has assessed depth.

stem: You are the ED registrar seeing a 3-year-old girl with 24 hours of profuse non-bilious vomiting and four watery stools over 12 hours. She has refused fluids for the last three hours and is tearful but consolable. Mum has been trying small sips of oral rehydration solution without success. She has no chronic illness, no surgical scars, and her abdomen is soft and non-tender. Capillary refill is two seconds and mucous membranes are slightly dry.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4°C, HR 132, RR 26, BP 96/60, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Alert, mild dryness of mucous membranes, capillary refill 2 seconds, soft abdomen, no peritonism, normal bowel sounds
data_table.Investigations: Bedside ketones 1.6 mmol/L, BSL 4.4 mmol/L; UEC not indicated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Insert IV line and commence a 20 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride bolus over 30 minutes now
options.rationale: IV fluid bolus is reserved for moderate-to-severe dehydration with haemodynamic compromise or failed oral rehydration after ondansetron; this child has only mild dehydration. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Nasogastric rehydration at 100 mL/kg oral rehydration solution given over four hours
options.rationale: NG rehydration is a reasonable option if oral fluids continue to fail after ondansetron, but it is not the first step in a child not yet trialled on ondansetron. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Send stool for viral PCR and bacterial culture before any further symptomatic treatment
options.rationale: Stool studies do not change acute management of typical viral gastroenteritis and are reserved for bloody diarrhoea, prolonged or atypical illness, or immunocompromise. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Single oral ondansetron 2 mg wafer followed by 30 minutes of patient oral rehydration
options.rationale: A single oral ondansetron dose (2 mg for 8-15 kg, 4 mg for over 15 kg) reduces vomiting and the need for IV fluids in mild-moderate dehydration; oral rehydration is then attempted before escalating. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Oral loperamide 50 micrograms/kg with each loose stool and discharge home with review
options.rationale: Antimotility agents are contraindicated in paediatric gastroenteritis because of toxic megacolon and CNS toxicity risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

explanation.summary: Mild to moderately dehydrated children with acute gastroenteritis who fail to keep down oral rehydration benefit from a single oral wafer dose of ondansetron, which roughly halves the rate of IV fluid escalation. If oral rehydration after ondansetron fails, NG rehydration is the next step, then IV fluids. Antimotility agents are not used in children and stool studies are reserved for atypical illness.
key_points: Ondansetron 2 mg wafer for 8-15 kg, 4 mg for over 15 kg, single dose.
Trial oral rehydration solution after ondansetron before considering IV or NG.
Avoid loperamide in children.
explanation.pearls: A single ondansetron wafer plus 30 minutes of patient oral rehydration prevents IV cannulation in roughly half of vomiting children with mild dehydration.

stem: You are the GP seeing the parents of a 3-year-old boy who for the last 6 weeks has woken about 90 minutes after sleep onset screaming, sweating, with dilated pupils and inconsolability. The episodes last 5 to 10 minutes, after which he settles into sleep. The next morning he has no memory of the event. He is meeting all developmental milestones, attends day care without concerns, and family history is unremarkable.

data_table.Examination: Normal neurological examination, growth on the 50th centile, no dysmorphic features

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Reassure parents, sleep hygiene advice, scheduled awakening 15 minutes before the usual time
options.rationale: Night terrors are a non-REM parasomnia that peaks at 3 to 7 years; reassurance, predictable bedtime routine and pre-emptive scheduled awakening for clustered episodes is first-line management, and most children outgrow them by adolescence. (Source: RACGP HANDI: Scheduled awakening for sleep terrors)
source_refs: RACGP HANDI: Scheduled awakening for sleep terrors

options.text: Refer to child psychiatry for assessment of an underlying primary anxiety disorder driver
options.rationale: Night terrors are not a manifestation of anxiety in toddlers and do not predict psychopathology; psychiatry referral is not indicated based on this history. (Source: RACGP HANDI)
source_refs: RACGP HANDI: Scheduled awakening for sleep terrors

options.text: Sleep-deprived EEG to exclude frontal lobe nocturnal seizures before any other treatment
options.rationale: EEG is reserved for atypical features such as stereotyped movements, daytime symptoms, very brief episodes or episodes later in the night; a typical 3-year-old parasomnia in the first third of the night does not need investigation. (Source: RACGP HANDI)
source_refs: RACGP HANDI: Scheduled awakening for sleep terrors

options.text: Commence low-dose melatonin 1 mg at bedtime and review clinical effect in four weeks time
options.rationale: Melatonin has weak evidence for sleep-onset insomnia in children, not for parasomnias, and is not the first-line approach for night terrors. (Source: AAP Clinical Report: Melatonin)

options.text: Prescribe oral clonazepam 0.05 mg/kg at bedtime for four weeks with monthly clinic review
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are reserved for severe, injury-causing parasomnias in older children after non-drug strategies have failed; routine use in a 3-year-old is not appropriate. (Source: AASM practice parameters)

explanation.summary: Night terrors are a non-REM parasomnia that occur in the first third of the night, peak between 3 and 7 years of age, and resolve spontaneously. Children appear awake but are unresponsive, have no recall, and should not be roused during the event. Management is reassurance, sleep hygiene and, for clustered episodes, scheduled awakening 15 minutes before the usual time. Investigations and pharmacotherapy are reserved for atypical or injurious episodes.
key_points: Typical onset 90 minutes after sleep, no recall, full recovery between episodes.
First-line management is reassurance and scheduled awakening.
Investigate only if features atypical (daytime, stereotyped, later in night).
explanation.pearls: Scheduled awakening 15 minutes before the expected terror, for two weeks, breaks the non-REM cycle and resolves clusters in most preschoolers.

sources.label: RACGP HANDI: Scheduled awakening for sleep terrors

stem:
You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 6-year-old boy with two days of dark cola-coloured urine and puffy eyes on waking. Two weeks ago he had impetigo on his legs that resolved with topical mupirocin. He has had a brief headache today but no seizures or visual changes. He is otherwise well, normotensive in the GP rooms (BP 105/65), and his urine dipstick shows 3+ blood and 1+ protein.

Bloods show normal urea and creatinine but low C3, normal C4, and a positive ASOT. There is no rash, no joint pain and no oliguria.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8°C, HR 96, RR 20, BP 118/78 in clinic on repeat
data_table.Examination: Mild periorbital oedema, no peripheral oedema, no rash, chest clear, no hepatosplenomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis 3+ blood 1+ protein, urine microscopy red cell casts, Cr 50 micromol/L, urea 5 mmol/L, C3 0.4 g/L (low), C4 0.2 g/L (normal), ASOT positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Arrange urgent renal biopsy to define the histological subtype before any other management
options.rationale: Biopsy is reserved for atypical features such as persistent low C3 beyond 3 months, normal complement, nephrotic-range proteinuria, or progressive renal impairment; this child has classic PSGN. (Source: KHA-CARI)

options.text: Admit for blood pressure monitoring with salt restriction and short-acting nifedipine if needed
options.rationale: Children with post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis are at risk of hypertensive encephalopathy and pulmonary oedema during the first 1 to 2 weeks; inpatient observation with salt restriction and short-acting antihypertensives like nifedipine is first-line if hypertension develops. (Source: KHA-CARI Adolescent and adult glomerulonephritis)

options.text: Reassure and discharge home on no specific therapy with weekly GP review for the next six weeks
options.rationale: Outpatient follow-up alone fails to capture the peak risk of hypertensive encephalopathy and pulmonary oedema in the first 1 to 2 weeks; admission for monitoring is safer. (Source: KHA-CARI)

options.text: Commence high-dose prednisolone 2 mg/kg daily for four weeks and outpatient nephrology review
options.rationale: PSGN is a self-limiting illness; corticosteroids are not part of standard management and are reserved for rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis or proven crescentic disease on biopsy. (Source: KHA-CARI)

options.text: Commence oral phenoxymethylpenicillin to eradicate residual streptococcal carriage at home
options.rationale: Antibiotics do not alter the course of established PSGN; if active streptococcal infection persists in a household it is treated for the index case, not as part of PSGN management. (Source: KHA-CARI: Glomerulonephritis)

explanation.summary: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis follows a streptococcal skin or pharyngeal infection by 1 to 3 weeks and presents with macroscopic haematuria, mild hypertension and periorbital oedema. Diagnosis is supported by low C3 with normal C4, evidence of recent streptococcal infection (ASOT or anti-DNase B), and red-cell casts on microscopy. Management is supportive with admission for BP and fluid monitoring during the first 1 to 2 weeks; complement and urine should normalise by 8 to 12 weeks.
key_points: Low C3, normal C4, red cell casts, positive ASOT or anti-DNase B suggest PSGN.
Inpatient monitoring focuses on BP and pulmonary congestion in the first 1-2 weeks.
Biopsy is reserved for atypical or progressive presentations.
explanation.pearls: If C3 remains low at 12 weeks after presumed PSGN, switch the diagnosis to MPGN or lupus nephritis and refer for biopsy.

stem: You are the paediatric registrar consulted about a 14-year-old girl with six weeks of daily holocephalic headache that is worse on waking and on coughing. She has had two episodes of transient visual obscurations. Her BMI is 32. She started oral isotretinoin two months ago for severe acne. There is no fever, neck stiffness, focal neurology or recent head injury. Fundoscopy shows bilateral papilloedema and visual fields show enlarged blind spots.

data_table.Examination: Alert, no focal neurology, fundoscopy bilateral disc swelling, no nuchal rigidity
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain and MR venogram: no mass, no venous sinus thrombosis, slit-like ventricles; lumbar puncture opening pressure 32 cm H2O lateral decubitus; CSF cells nil, protein 0.3 g/L, glucose normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Halve the isotretinoin dose and add propranolol 20 mg twice daily for migraine prophylaxis
options.rationale: Continuing the precipitant perpetuates the disease and the picture is not migraine; propranolol does not treat IIH. (Source: ANZCNS: Paediatric IIH)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily and ophthalmology review with perimetry in two weeks
options.rationale: Corticosteroids cause rebound intracranial hypertension on withdrawal and risk weight gain in an obese adolescent; they are not first-line. (Source: ANZCNS: Paediatric IIH)

options.text: Cease isotretinoin, start acetazolamide 500 mg twice daily, refer for serial automated perimetry
options.rationale: Stopping the precipitant (isotretinoin, tetracyclines, vitamin A excess), commencing acetazolamide and arranging serial automated perimetry is the standard AU adolescent management of idiopathic intracranial hypertension; surgical CSF diversion is for failing vision or medication-refractory disease. (Source: Australian and New Zealand Child Neurology Society IIH guidance)

options.text: Arrange therapeutic lumbar punctures every 48 hours to maintain a low CSF opening pressure
options.rationale: Serial therapeutic LPs are not the long-term management; they are uncomfortable and risky and have been replaced by acetazolamide and weight loss. (Source: ANZCNS: Paediatric IIH)

options.text: Urgent neurosurgical referral for ventriculoperitoneal shunt insertion before any other therapy
options.rationale: Shunting (lumboperitoneal or ventriculoperitoneal) and optic-nerve sheath fenestration are reserved for vision loss despite optimal medical therapy. (Source: ANZCNS: Paediatric IIH)

explanation.summary: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension in adolescents is associated with obesity and certain drugs including isotretinoin, tetracyclines, growth hormone and excess vitamin A. Diagnostic criteria include papilloedema, normal neuroimaging including venogram, and elevated CSF opening pressure with normal constituents. Management is precipitant removal, acetazolamide and weight loss, with surgical options reserved for vision loss despite medical therapy.
key_points: Cease the precipitant drug before starting other therapy.
Acetazolamide is first-line pharmacotherapy.
Serial perimetry monitors visual function; surgery if vision loss progresses.
explanation.pearls: Any teenager on isotretinoin or doxycycline with a new daily headache needs fundoscopy at the first visit; missing papilloedema risks irreversible optic atrophy.
